post_text: This is fundamental. The disruption index from Wang, Veugelers & Stephan finally gives us a quantitative way to detect when a paper actually changes a field versus when it's just incremental. Going to cite this in every grant.
paper_title: Disruption metric breaks down between disciplines
paper_abstract: We test the disruption index across STEM and social-science citation networks and find it captures a real distinction between consolidating and innovating work.

gold.claim_text: The disruption index reliably distinguishes consolidating from innovating papers across fields

post_text: Yeah this lines up with what I see in our citation data. Papers with high D scores really do get cited differently downstream.
paper_title: Disruption metric breaks down between disciplines
paper_abstract: We test the disruption index across STEM and social-science citation networks and find it captures a real distinction between consolidating and innovating work.

gold.claim_text: High disruption scores correlate with distinct downstream citation patterns

post_text: Plausible but I'd want to see it replicated on a non-WoS dataset before getting too excited.
paper_title: Disruption metric breaks down between disciplines
paper_abstract: We test the disruption index across STEM and social-science citation networks and find it captures a real distinction between consolidating and innovating work.

gold.claim_text: The disruption-index result holds across citation databases

post_text: Not convinced. They normalize by field but I think their field assignments are doing a lot of the work here. Reviewers should push back on this one.
paper_title: Disruption metric breaks down between disciplines
paper_abstract: We test the disruption index across STEM and social-science citation networks and find it captures a real distinction between consolidating and innovating work.

gold.claim_text: The disruption-index result is robust to field-normalization choices

post_text: This is exactly the kind of metric overreach that's killing scientometrics. They have no causal identification, their D index is sensitive to citation lag, and the cross-field comparison is nonsense. This paper should never have been published in this form.
paper_title: Disruption metric breaks down between disciplines
paper_abstract: We test the disruption index across STEM and social-science citation networks and find it captures a real distinction between consolidating and innovating work.

gold.claim_text: The disruption-index methodology in this paper is sound

post_text: Their sample of 5000 papers is tiny for this kind of cross-disciplinary claim. Statistical power is going to be a real issue.
paper_title: Gender gaps in peer review acceptance
paper_abstract: Analysis of 2.1M peer-reviewed manuscripts shows persistent acceptance-rate gaps between male and female first authors.

gold.rationale: Sample size too small for the cross-disciplinary claim being made

post_text: Pretty sure the original data behind Figure 3 was retracted last year. They should have known.
paper_title: Replication crisis in social psychology revisited
paper_abstract: We attempt to replicate 100 high-impact social psychology findings and report success rates by methodology.

post_text: Two of the senior authors sit on the editorial board of the journal that published this. Worth noting in any discussion.
paper_title: Open access mandates increase citation counts
paper_abstract: Causal inference on the effect of OA mandates on citation rates over 10 years of European Research Council grants.

gold.rationale: Authors have editorial-board conflict of interest with the publishing journal
